changed.from: Every \(Q\) satisfying \(\textup{ass:pushforward}\) obeys \(q_{t0}(X)=e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\) \(P_X\)-almost surely for each \(t\in\mathcal T\). Conditioning \(E_Q[Y(t)\mid X,S=0]\) on \(A\), the pushforward identifies the weights with \(e_k\), its realized-outcome coordinate identifies the \(A=t\) conditional mean with \(\mu_{0t}\), and the other conditional mean is \(c_t\).
changed.to: Every \(Q\) satisfying \(\textup{ass:pushforward}\) obeys the weighted observational-mixture identity\[(1-\pi(X))\bigl[q_{t0}(X)-e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)-e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\bigr]=0\]\(P_X\)-almost surely for each \(t\in\mathcal T\). Consequently, the unweighted identity holds \(P_X\)-almost surely on \(\{x:\pi(x)<1\}\).
changed.reason: Without observational-study positivity, conditioning on \(S=0\) determines conditional means only \(P_{X\mid S=0}\)-almost surely. The pushforward implies the weighted identity, but it cannot determine the unweighted equality on strata with \(P(S=0\mid X)=0\).

changed.from: Under \(\textup{ass:uniform-treatment-positivity}\), \(C_P(\theta)>0\) implies \(\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)=\varnothing\). Indeed, any \(Q\in\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\) has \(c_t\in[C_t^-,C_t^+]\); Lemma 1 then gives \(q_{t0}\in[W_t^-,W_t^+]\), while study exchangeability gives \(q_{t0}\in[V_t^-,V_t^+]\). Thus every nonnegative conditional violation is zero almost surely.
changed.to: Under \(\textup{ass:uniform-study-positivity}\) and \(\textup{ass:uniform-treatment-positivity}\), \(C_P(\theta)>0\) implies \(\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)=\varnothing\). Indeed, for any \(Q\in\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\), the weighted observational-mixture identity may be divided by \(1-\pi(X)\ge\varepsilon\). Since \(c_t\in[C_t^-,C_t^+]\), it follows that \(q_{t0}\in[W_t^-,W_t^+]\), while study exchangeability gives \(q_{t0}\in[V_t^-,V_t^+]\). Thus every nonnegative conditional violation is zero almost surely, contradicting \(C_P(\theta)>0\).
changed.reason: The claimed \(P_X\)-almost-sure mixture bridge requires positive observational-study mass. Uniform treatment positivity does not supply this missing division. The added condition is already frozen and is also imposed by the exact characterization.

changed.from:
{"id":"lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary","kind":"lemma","statement":"The primitive conditions admit the following specialization for every \(d\ge1\) and \(\beta>d/2\): take \(\mathcal X=[0,1]^d\), \(X\) uniform, \(S\) and \(A\) independent fair Bernoulli variables, and an interior baseline mean \(m_0=(a+b)/2\), with \(B\) and the density bounds chosen to contain this submodel. Put \(Z=(Y-a)/(b-a)\). For a function \(u:[0,1]^d\to[-1/4,1/4]\), let \(P_u\) have \(Z\) Bernoulli with conditional success probability \(1/2+u(X)\) when \(S=1,A=0\), and success probability \(1/2\) in every other study-arm cell. Restrict \(u\) to a fixed bounded \(C^\beta\)-ball small enough that the resulting outcome regressions lie in \(\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\). Then\[C_{P_u}(0,0)=(b-a)\lVert u\rVert_{L_1([0,1]^d)}.\]Let \(r_n=n^{-2\beta/(4\beta+d)}\). For every \(\eta>0\), there is \(c_\eta>0\) such that every test \(\varphi_n\) with \(E_{P_0}\varphi_n\le\alpha+o(1)\) has, for some \(P_{u_n}\),\[C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)\asymp c_\eta r_n,\qquad E_{P_{u_n}}\varphi_n\le\alpha+\eta+o(1).\]There likewise exist alternatives with \(C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)\asymp n^{-1/2}\) and power at most \(\alpha+o(1)\). Conversely, let \(p_1,\ldots,p_K\) be an orthonormal piecewise-polynomial basis of order at least \(\lceil\beta\rceil\), put \(K\asymp n^{2d/(4\beta+d)}\), define \[R_i=4\{1\{S_i=1,A_i=0\}-1\{S_i=0,A_i=0\}\}Z_i,\qquad \widehat Q_K=\frac{1}{n(n-1)}\sum_{i\ne j}R_iR_j\sum_{k=1}^Kp_k(X_i)p_k(X_j),\]and reject for \(\widehat Q_K>L_\alpha\sqrt K/n\), where \(L_\alpha\) is a sufficiently large constant. This test has level at most \(\alpha\) under \(P_0\) and power tending to one uniformly over the displayed Hölder submodel whenever \(C_{P_u}(0,0)/r_n\to\infty\). Thus \(r_n\) is the sharp minimax separation order in this positive-mass arm-and-endpoint-tie subexperiment.","depends_on":["ass:iid","def:endpoint-kernel","def:exact-endpoints","def:compatibility-functional","def:primitive-class"],"proof_tex":"We first verify membership and compute the target. The chosen study and treatment probabilities equal \(1/2\), so both positivity assumptions hold for every \(\varepsilon<1/2\). The covariate density is one. The endpoint law of \(Y\) is \(\nu_m\) with \(m=m_0+(b-a)u(x)\) in the cell \(S=1,A=0\) and \(m=m_0\) elsewhere. Choosing the radius of the \(C^\beta\)-ball for \(u\) sufficiently small keeps every mean in \([a,b]\) and every nuisance in the prescribed Hölder ball. Thus these laws form an admissible specialization of \(\overline{\mathcal P}\).

At \(\rho=\gamma=0\), support clipping is inactive because every conditional mean belongs to \([a,b]\), and the binary-treatment identity \(e_0(x,s)+e_1(x,s)=1\) gives\[C_t^-(x;0)=C_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{0t}(x),\quad V_t^-(x;0)=V_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{1t}(x),\quad W_t^-(x;0)=W_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{0t}(x).\]Consequently, the conditional violation for arm \(t\) is \(\lvert\mu_{1t}(x)-\mu_{0t}(x)\rvert\). Only arm zero differs in the submodel, whence\[C_{P_u}(0,0)=(b-a)\int_{[0,1]^d}|u(x)|\,dx.\]All four faces and the positive part tie on the whole covariate support at \(u=0\), so the tie has positive, indeed unit, \(P_X\)-mass.

We next prove the lower bound by an explicit mixture. Choose a nonzero nonnegative \(C^\infty\) function \(\psi\), supported in the interior of \([0,1]^d\), with \(\lVert\psi\rVert_\infty\le1\). For a small bandwidth \(h\), pack \(M\asymp h^{-d}\) disjoint cubes of side \(h\) into \([0,1]^d\), with lower corners \(x_1,\ldots,x_M\). For \(\omega=(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_M)\in\{-1,1\}^M\), set\[u_\omega(x)=c h^\beta\sum_{j=1}^M\omega_j\psi\!\left(\frac{x-x_j}{h}\right).\]The supports are disjoint. The scaling identity for derivatives and the Hölder remainder show directly that \(\lVert u_\omega\rVert_{C^\beta}\le C_\psi c\), uniformly in \(h\) and \(\omega\). Taking \(c\) small therefore places every alternative in the submodel and in \([-1/4,1/4]\). Moreover,\[\lVert u_\omega\rVert_1=c h^\beta M h^d\int|\psi(z)|\,dz\asymp c h^\beta.\]

Let \(L_\omega=dP_{u_\omega}/dP_0\) for one observation. Conditional on \(G=1\{S=1,A=0\}\), the Bernoulli likelihood ratio is \(1+2(2Z-1)u_\omega(X)\), and it is one when \(G=0\). Since \(P_0(G=1)=1/4\), \(E_{P_0}(2Z-1)=0\), and \((2Z-1)^2=1\), direct multiplication gives\[E_{P_0}[L_\omega L_{\omega'}]=1+\langle u_\omega,u_{\omega'}\rangle_2.\]Let \(\overline P_n=2^{-M}\sum_\omega P_{u_\omega}^{\otimes n}\). Orthogonality of the disjoint bumps gives, for a constant \(\lambda=c^2\int\psi^2\),\[\langle u_\omega,u_{\omega'}\rangle_2=\lambda h^{2\beta+d}\sum_{j=1}^M\omega_j\omega_j'.\]Using \((1+x)^n\le e^{nx}\), averaging independently over \(\omega\) and \(\omega'\), and using \(\log\cosh z\le z^2/2\), we obtain\[1+\chi^2(\overline P_n,P_0^{\otimes n})\le\left\{\cosh\bigl(n\lambda h^{2\beta+d}\bigr)\right\}^M\le\exp\left(\frac{M n^2\lambda^2h^{4\beta+2d}}2\right)\le\exp(Cc^4n^2h^{4\beta+d}).\]Take \(h\asymp n^{-2/(4\beta+d)}\). Then the last exponent is bounded by a constant multiple of \(c^4\), while every alternative has \(C_{P_{u_\omega}}(0,0)\asymp c(b-a)r_n\). By choosing \(c=c_\eta\) sufficiently small, the total-variation inequality \(\lVert\overline P_n-P_0^{\otimes n}\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\le\sqrt{\chi^2(\overline P_n,P_0^{\otimes n})}/2\) makes this distance at most \(\eta+o(1)\). {"id":"lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary","kind":"lemma","statement":"The primitive conditions admit the following specialization for every \(d\ge1\) and \(\beta>d/2\): take \(\mathcal X=[0,1]^d\), \(X\) uniform, \(S\) and \(A\) independent fair Bernoulli variables, and an interior baseline mean \(m_0=(a+b)/2\), with \(B\) and the density bounds chosen to contain this submodel. For a function \(u:[0,1]^d\to[-1/4,1/4]\), let \(P_u\) have \(Z\) Bernoulli with conditional success probability \(1/2+u(X)\) when \(S=1,A=0\), and success probability \(1/2\) in every other study-arm cell. Restrict \(u\) to a fixed bounded \(C^\beta\)-ball small enough that the resulting outcome regressions lie in \(\mathcal H_B^\beta(\mathcal X)\). Then\[C_{P_u}(0,0)=(b-a)\lVert u\rVert_{L_1([0,1]^d)}.\]Let \(r_n=n^{-2\beta/(4\beta+d)}\). For every \(\eta>0\), there is \(c_\eta>0\) such that every test \(\varphi_n\) with \(E_{P_0}\varphi_n\le\alpha+o(1)\) has, for some \(P_{u_n}\),\[C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)\asymp c_\eta r_n,\qquad E_{P_{u_n}}\varphi_n\le\alpha+\eta+o(1).\]There likewise exist alternatives with \(C_{P_{u_n}}(0,0)\asymp n^{-1/2}\) and power at most \(\alpha+o(1)\). Conversely, let \(p_1,\ldots,p_K\) be an orthonormal piecewise-polynomial basis of order at least \(\lceil\beta\rceil\), put \(K\asymp n^{2d/(4\beta+d)}\), define \[R_i=4\{1\{S_i=1,A_i=0\}-1\{S_i=0,A_i=0\}\}Z_i,\qquad \widehat Q_K=\frac{1}{n(n-1)}\sum_{i\ne j}R_iR_j\sum_{k=1}^Kp_k(X_i)p_k(X_j),\]and reject for \(\widehat Q_K>L_\alpha\sqrt K/n\), where \(L_\alpha\) is a sufficiently large constant. This test has level at most \(\alpha\) under \(P_0\) and power tending to one uniformly over the displayed Hölder submodel whenever \(C_{P_u}(0,0)/r_n\to\infty\). Thus \(r_n\) is the sharp minimax separation order in this positive-mass arm-and-endpoint-tie subexperiment.","depends_on":["ass:iid","def:endpoint-kernel","def:exact-endpoints","def:compatibility-functional","def:primitive-class"],"status":"proved","proof_tex":"We first verify membership and compute the target. The chosen study and treatment probabilities equal \(1/2\), so both positivity assumptions hold for every \(\varepsilon<1/2\). The covariate density is one. The endpoint law of \(Y\) is \(\nu_m\) with \(m=m_0+(b-a)u(x)\) in the cell \(S=1,A=0\) and \(m=m_0\) elsewhere. Choosing the radius of the \(C^\beta\)-ball for \(u\) sufficiently small keeps every mean in \([a,b]\) and every nuisance in the prescribed Hölder ball. At \(\rho=\gamma=0\), support clipping is inactive because every conditional mean belongs to \([a,b]\), and the binary-treatment identity \(e_0(x,s)+e_1(x,s)=1\) gives\[C_t^-(x;0)=C_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{0t}(x),\quad V_t^-(x;0)=V_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{1t}(x),\quad W_t^-(x;0)=W_t^+(x;0)=\mu_{0t}(x).\]Consequently, the conditional violation for arm \(t\) is \(\lvert\mu_{1t}(x)-\mu_{0t}(x)\rvert\). Only arm zero differs in the submodel, whence\[C_{P_u}(0,0)=(b-a)\int_{[0,1]^d}|u(x)|\,dx.\]All four faces and the positive part tie on the whole covariate support at \(u=0\), so the tie has positive, indeed unit, \(P_X\)-mass.

We next prove the lower bound by an explicit mixture. Choose a nonzero nonnegative \(C^\infty\) function \(\psi\), supported in the interior of \([0,1]^d\), with \(\lVert\psi\rVert_\infty\le1\). For a small bandwidth \(h\), pack \(M\asymp h^{-d}\) disjoint cubes of side \(h\) into \([0,1]^d\), with lower corners \(x_1,\ldots,x_M\). For \(\omega=(\omega_1,\ldots,\omega_M)\in\{-1,1\}^M\), set\[u_\omega(x)=c h^\beta\sum_{j=1}^M\omega_j\psi\!\left(\frac{x-x_j}{h}\right).\]The supports are disjoint. The scaling identity for derivatives and the Hölder remainder show directly that \(\lVert u_\omega\rVert_{C^\beta}\le C_\psi c\), uniformly in \(h\) and \(\omega\). Taking \(c\) small therefore places every alternative in the submodel and in \([-1/4,1/4]\). Moreover,\[\lVert u_\omega\rVert_1=c h^\beta M h^d\int|\psi(z)|\,dz\asymp c h^\beta.\]

Let \(L_\omega=dP_{u_\omega}/dP_0\) for one observation. Conditional on \(G=1\{S=1,A=0\}\), the Bernoulli likelihood ratio is \(1+2(2Z-1)u_\omega(X)\), and it is one when \(G=0\). Orthogonality of the disjoint bumps gives, for a constant \(\lambda=c^2\int\psi^2\),\[\langle u_\omega,u_{\omega'}\rangle_2=\lambda h^{2\beta+d}\sum_{j=1}^M\omega_j\omega_j'.\]Using \((1+x)^n\le e^{nx}\), averaging independently over \(\omega\) and \(\omega'\), and using \(\log\cosh z\le z^2/2\), we obtain\[1+\chi^2(\overline P_n,P_0^{\otimes n})\le\left\{\cosh\bigl(n\lambda h^{2\beta+d}\bigr)\right\}^M\le\exp\left(\frac{M n^2\lambda^2h^{4\beta+2d}}2\right)\le\exp(Cc^4n^2h^{4\beta+d}).\]Take \(h\asymp n^{-2/(4\beta+d)}\). Then the last exponent is bounded by a constant multiple of \(c^4\), while every alternative has \(C_{P_{u_\omega}}(0,0)\asymp c(b-a)r_n\). By choosing \(c=c_\eta\) sufficiently small, the total-variation inequality \(\lVert\overline P_n-P_0^{\otimes n}\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\le\sqrt{\chi^2(\overline P_n,P_0^{\otimes n})}/2\) makes this distance at most \(\eta+o(1)\). Therefore\[2^{-M}\sum_\omega E_{P_{u_\omega}}\varphi_n\le E_{P_0}\varphi_n+\eta+o(1)\le\alpha+\eta+o(1),\]so at least one mixture component has the asserted low power. If instead \(h\asymp n^{-1/(2\beta)}\), then the signal is of order \(n^{-1/2}\) and \(n^2h^{4\beta+d}=n^{-d/(2\beta)}\to0\). The same calculation gives total variation tending to zero and hence power at most \(\alpha+o(1)\) for at least one root-\(n\) alternative.

It remains to prove attainability of the rate. Define the projection kernel \(K_K(x,x')=\sum_{k=1}^Kp_k(x)p_k(x')\). The inverse-cell weight in \(R_i\) gives\[E_{P_u}[R_i\mid X_i=x]=4\left\{\frac14\left(\frac12+u(x)\right)-\frac14\left(\frac12\right)\right\}=u(x).\]Independence of two distinct observations therefore yields\[E_{P_u}[\widehat Q_K]=\sum_{k=1}^K\langle u,p_k\rangle_2^2=\lVert\Pi_Ku\rVert_2^2.\]A piecewise Taylor polynomial of degree \(\lfloor\beta\rfloor\) on cubes of side proportional to \(K^{-1/d}\) has pointwise remainder at most \(C K^{-\beta/d}\) by the defining Hölder inequality. Since the orthogonal projection is the best \(L_2\) approximation,\[\lVert u-\Pi_Ku\rVert_2\le C K^{-\beta/d}.\]Also \(|R_i|\le4\). Writing the order-two statistic as its first projection plus a degenerate remainder and expanding the variance gives\[\operatorname{Var}_{P_u}(\widehat Q_K)\le C\left\{\frac{\lVert\Pi_Ku\rVert_2^2}{n}+\frac{E[K_K(X_1,X_2)^2]}{n^2}\right\}=C\left\{\frac{\lVert\Pi_Ku\rVert_2^2}{n}+\frac K{n^2}\right\}.\]The equality uses orthonormality twice: \(E[K_K(X_1,X_2)^2]=K\). Under \(u=0\), Chebyshev's inequality thus makes the rejection probability at most \(C/L_\alpha^2\), so a fixed sufficiently large \(L_\alpha\) gives level at most \(\alpha\). On a unit-volume cube, \(\lVert u\rVert_2\ge\lVert u\rVert_1\). With \(K\asymp n^{2d/(4\beta+d)}\),\[K^{-\beta/d}\asymp r_n,\qquad \frac{\sqrt K}{n}\asymp r_n^2.\]Hence \(\lVert u\rVert_1/r_n\to\infty\) implies \(E\widehat Q_K/(\sqrt K/n)\to\infty\), and the displayed variance bound plus Chebyshev's inequality gives rejection probability tending to one uniformly. This proves the matching upper bound and the lemma."}

{"id":"thm:uniform-contact-coverage-and-level","kind":"theorem","statement":"Choose the multipliers in \(\textup{def:inference-handle}\) to be independent of the data and i.i.d. standard Gaussian or i.i.d. Rademacher. Under \(P\in\overline{\mathcal P}\), \(\textup{ass:iid}\), and all construction-performance conditions in \(\textup{def:inference-handle}\), the numerical directional-delta procedure is uniformly one-sided valid, without any uniqueness or margin condition on the endpoint, positive-part, or arm maximizers. They assert coverage and level only: by \(\textup{thm:tie-local-separation-obstruction}\), they cannot be strengthened to a nontrivial uniform power curve indexed only by \(\sqrt n C_P(\theta)\) over the same tie-inclusive class.","depends_on":["ass:iid","def:exact-endpoints","def:compatibility-functional","def:primitive-class","def:inference-handle","lem:hong-li-convex-numerical-delta","lem:tie-subexperiment-minimax-boundary","lem:regular-constant-path-power","thm:tie-local-separation-obstruction"],"proof_tex":"Fix an arbitrary sequence \(P_n\in\overline{\mathcal P}\). Proving the asserted bounds along every such sequence proves uniformity: if either uniform bound failed, a subsequence of laws witnessing that failure would contradict the corresponding sequential conclusion below. All stochastic orders in the proof are therefore uniform along this arbitrary sequence.

First expose the finite max representation. For \(r\in\mathcal R=\{11,12,21,22\}\), define\[\begin{aligned}d_{t,11}&=V_t^- -V_t^+, & d_{t,12}&=V_t^- -W_t^+,\\d_{t,21}&=W_t^- -V_t^+, & d_{t,22}&=W_t^- -W_t^+.\end{aligned}\]All functions in this display are evaluated at \((x;\rho,\gamma)\). Elementary max--min algebra gives, pointwise in \(x\),\[\left(\max\{V_t^-,W_t^-\}-\min\{V_t^+,W_t^+\}\right)_+=\max\left\{0,d_{t,r}:r\in\mathcal R\right\}.\tag{1}\]Thus \(C_{P_n}\) is obtained from the vector of elementary faces by finitely many coordinatewise maxima, linear expectation, and one final maximum over \(t\). Every coordinatewise maximum is convex and one-Lipschitz in the sup norm; expectation is linear; and a maximum of convex functions is convex. Consequently the complete map \(\mathfrak F\) is convex and Lipschitz, with a constant depending only on the fixed number of faces and not on \(n\), \(P_n\), or the tie pattern. The support clips do not change this conclusion: a lower clip is a maximum of two affine branches, while minus an upper clip is a maximum of two affine branches. The products in \(W_t^\pm\) are smooth between branch changes; their first-order remainders are products of nuisance errors. This is precisely the convex numerical-delta setting of \(\textup{lem:hong-li-convex-numerical-delta}\).

We next verify the required uniform first-order reduction from the primitive construction. Condition on the training samples of the fixed cross-fitting folds. Positivity in \(\textup{def:primitive-class}\) bounds every inverse probability in \(R^\mu_{sk}\) and \(R^e_{sk}\), and bounded outcomes bound every residual. At the true nuisances, iterated expectation gives\[E_{P_n}[R^\mu_{sk}\mid X]=0,\qquad E_{P_n}[R^e_{sk}\mid X]=0.\tag{2}\]For any fixed path through the lower maximum, upper minimum, support clips, positive part, and outer arm maximum, Taylor expansion of its smooth pieces and the identities in (2) give the cross-fitted expansion\[\widehat M_{n,a}(\theta)-M_{P_n,a}(\theta)=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n s_{n,a,\theta}(O_i)+R_{n,a}(\theta),\tag{3}\]where \(s_{n,a,\theta}\) is the centered active score obtained by following path \(a\). For a clipped branch the expansion is exact on that affine branch. For \(W_t^\pm\), the only nonlinear remainder is bounded, for a constant depending only on \(a,b,\varepsilon\), by\[C r_{\mu,n}(r_{e,n}+r_{\pi,n})+C K_n^{-2\beta/d}.\tag{4}\]The first term in (4) is \(o(n^{-1/2})\) by the stated product-rate condition. The second is \(o(n^{-1/2})\) because \(K_n^{-\beta/d}=o(n^{-1/4})\). Cross-fitting makes the evaluation empirical process conditionally independent of each training fit. The condition \(K_n\log n/n=o(1)\), the stated foldwise errors, and the envelope and entropy bounds for \(\mathcal S_n^{\mathrm{act}}\) then make the empirical fluctuation of the estimated-minus-oracle scores \(o_{P_n}(1)\) after root-\(n\) scaling. Hence\[\sup_{a,\theta}\sqrt n\,|R_{n,a}(\theta)|=o_{P_n}(1).\tag{5}\]

For completeness, the empirical-process assertion used in (5) and below follows directly from the displayed performance conditions. At \(L_2(P_n)\)-radius \(u\), the active-score class has a net of cardinality at most \((A_0/u)^v\). On each fixed net, truncation at \(n^{1/q}\), the ordinary multivariate Lindeberg argument, and \(q>4\) give the joint Gaussian approximation; the same calculation conditional on the data gives the Gaussian- or Rademacher-multiplier approximation. The entropy integral\[\int_0^1\sqrt{v\log(A_0/u)}\,du<\infty\tag{6}\]and the \(L_q\) envelope bound make the oscillations between net points vanish uniformly as the radius tends to zero. Uniform laws of large numbers for products of active scores make the empirical covariance kernel consistent. It follows, by first letting \(n\to\infty\) on a fixed net and then letting the net radius tend to zero, that the empirical and conditional multiplier suprema over any active subclass have the same Gaussian subsequential limits. The fixed variance floor supplies Gaussian anti-concentration for every nonzero-variance coordinate; the stipulated deterministic treatment of zero-variance scores handles the remaining coordinates. This proves the uniform quantile comparison required below, including at distributions with an atom at zero when generalized quantiles are used and rejection is strict.

Let\[\mathcal N_n(P_n)=\left\{\theta\in\Theta_n:\operatorname{dist}(\theta,\mathcal K(P_n))\le h_n\right\}.\]The interpolation condition in \(\textup{def:inference-handle}\) implies\[\sup_{\theta\in\mathcal N_n(P_n)}\sqrt n\,C_{P_n}(\theta)=o(1).\tag{7}\]For every \(\theta\) with \(C_{P_n}(\theta)=0\), (1) and nonnegativity imply that every arm has zero integrated violation and that every pointwise violation is zero \(P_{n,X}\)-almost surely. More generally, for \(\theta\in\mathcal N_n(P_n)\), the population mean of every branch selected by the nested maxima is at most \(C_{P_n}(\theta)\). Maximizing (3) over the branch paths that the estimator can select, and using (5)--(7), therefore yields\[T_n:=\sup_{\theta\in\mathcal N_n(P_n)}\sqrt n\,\widehat C_n(\theta)\le Z_n+o_{P_n}(1),\qquad Z_n:=\sup_{s\in\mathcal A_n(P_n)}\frac1{\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n s(O_i),\tag{8}\]for an active subclass \(\mathcal A_n(P_n)\subseteq\mathcal S_n^{\mathrm{act}}\). The class retains every tied branch by construction. Notice that (8) uses no separation between a winning and a losing face.

It remains to compare the bootstrap critical value with \(Z_n\). The elementary deterministic inequality behind the numerical delta method is the following. If \(f\) is convex, \(r\delta\ge1\), and \(z,g\) belong to its domain, then\[r\{f(z+g/r)-f(z)\}\le\frac{f(z+\delta g)-f(z)}{\delta}.\tag{9}\]Indeed, \(z+g/r=(1-1/(r\delta))z+(1/(r\delta))(z+\delta g)\), so (9) is exactly the defining convexity inequality. Also, if \(\widehat z\) replaces \(z\), Lipschitz continuity bounds the change in the right-hand side of (9) by \(2L\lVert\widehat z-z\rVert/\delta\). Here \(r=\sqrt n\), \(n^{-1/2}/\delta_n\to0\), and the foldwise nuisance-error condition gives\[\frac{\lVert\widehat z-z\rVert}{\delta_n}=o_{P_n}(1).\tag{10}\]Applying (9) successively to the support clips, lower and upper extrema, positive part, expectation, and outer maximum, and using (10), shows conditionally on the data that\[\sup_{\theta\in\Theta_n}\widehat C_n^\xi(\theta)\ge Z_n^\xi-o_{P_n}(1),\qquad Z_n^\xi:=\sup_{s\in\mathcal A_n(P_n)}\frac1{\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n\xi_i\widehat s_i.\tag{11}\]This branch-following inequality is where tie retention matters: if several faces tie, every one of them remains available on the right-hand side of the finite difference. Since the estimation error is \(o(\delta_n)\), no genuinely active branch can be discarded by an estimated gap. Positive-mass ties therefore create no additional remainder.

By the uniform empirical and multiplier comparison following (6), by (11), and by the generalized-quantile conclusion of \(\textup{lem:hong-li-convex-numerical-delta}\), the conditional \((1-\alpha)\)-quantile \(\widehat c_{1-\alpha}\) satisfies\[\limsup_{n\to\infty}P_n\left(Z_n>\widehat c_{1-\alpha}+o_{P_n}(1)\right)\le\alpha.\tag{12}\]Combining (8) and (12) gives\[\liminf_{n\to\infty}P_n\left(\sqrt n\,\widehat C_n(\theta)\le\widehat c_{1-\alpha}\ 	ext{for every }\theta\in\mathcal N_n(P_n)\right)\ge1-\alpha.\tag{13}\]

Every point of \(\mathcal K(P_n)\) lies in a closed grid cell represented by a point of \(\mathcal N_n(P_n)\). Therefore the closed-cell enlargement in \(\textup{def:inference-handle}\) turns the event in (13) into\[\mathcal K(P_n)\subseteq\widehat{\mathcal K}_{n,1-\alpha}.\]Since \(P_n\) was an arbitrary sequence, the asserted uniform coverage follows. If \(C_P(\theta)=0\), then \(\theta\in\mathcal K(P)\); rejection of that null implies exclusion of \(\theta\) from the inverted region. Taking the supremum over \(P\) and null \(\theta\) in the already proved simultaneous bound yields the uniform level statement. Finally, \(\textup{thm:tie-local-separation-obstruction}\) shows why this valid but possibly conservative procedure has no uniform root-\(n\) local-power consequence over positive-mass ties, so the coverage and level result is consistent with the established minimax separation boundary.","status":"proved"}

By the pushforward restriction, on an observational arm with positive conditional probability,\[E_Q\{Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=t\}=E_P\{Y\mid X,S=0,A=t\}=\mu_{0t}(X).\]By definition,\[E_Q\{Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=1-t\}=c_t(X).\]Conditioning first on \(A\) therefore gives\[q_{t0}(X)=e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t(X)\qquad P_{X\mid S=0}\text{-almost surely}.\]The study-positivity inequality \(1-\pi(X)\ge\varepsilon\) makes \(P_{X\mid S=0}\) and \(P_X\) equivalent. Hence the last display holds \(P_X\)-almost surely. This argument also shows explicitly why division by observational-study mass is required.

The relative-NUC restriction and the nonnegativity of the treatment probabilities imply\[\begin{aligned}e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)C_t^-(X;\rho)&\le q_{t0}(X)\\&\le e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)C_t^+(X;\rho).\end{aligned}\]By the definition of the exact endpoints, this is\[q_{t0}(X)\in[W_t^-(X;\rho),W_t^+(X;\rho)]\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Study exchangeability also gives\[q_{t0}(X)\in[V_t^-(X;\gamma),V_t^+(X;\gamma)]\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Thus the two closed intervals intersect almost surely, so\[\max\{V_t^-(X;\gamma),W_t^-(X;\rho)\}\le\min\{V_t^+(X;\gamma),W_t^+(X;\rho)\}\]for each \(t\). Every positive-part violation is zero almost surely, and therefore \(C_P(\theta)=0\).

\emph{Sufficiency.} Suppose \(C_P(\theta)=0\), and put\[\ell_t(x)=\max\{V_t^-(x;\gamma),W_t^-(x;\rho)\},\qquad u_t(x)=\min\{V_t^+(x;\gamma),W_t^+(x;\rho)\}.\]For \(D_t(X)=(\ell_t(X)-u_t(X))_+\), the definition of \(C_P\) yields \(E_{P_X}D_t=0\) for every \(t\), because all of these expectations are nonnegative and their finite maximum is zero. Hence \(D_t=0\) almost surely. On the resulting common full-measure set, define\[q_t^*(x)=\frac{\ell_t(x)+u_t(x)}2.\]Then\[q_t^*(x)\in[V_t^-(x;\gamma),V_t^+(x;\gamma)]\cap[W_t^-(x;\rho),W_t^+(x;\rho)].\]Since \(e_{1-t}(x,0)\ge\varepsilon\), set\[c_t^*(x)=\frac{q_t^*(x)-e_t(x,0)\mu_{0t}(x)}{e_{1-t}(x,0)}.\]Membership of \(q_t^*\) in the \(W_t\)-interval and division by the positive coefficient \(e_{1-t}(x,0)\) give\[C_t^-(x;\rho)\le c_t^*(x)\le C_t^+(x;\rho).\]In particular, \(c_t^*(x)\in[a,b]\). All displayed selectors are measurable because they are obtained from measurable nuisance versions by finitely many maxima, minima, sums, and division by a function bounded away from zero. Modify them on the exceptional \(P_X\)-null set, if necessary, to take values in \([a,b]\).

It remains to construct one common latent law rather than merely compatible conditional means. By Lemma \(\textup{lem:standard-borel-conditional-kernel}\), choose measurable kernels \(\kappa_{st}(x,dy)\) that are versions of the conditional law of \(Y\) given \(X=x,S=s,A=t\). Positivity ensures\[\int y\,\kappa_{st}(x,dy)=\mu_{st}(x)\qquad P_X\text{-almost surely}.\]Write \(p_1(x)=\pi(x)\) and \(p_0(x)=1-\pi(x)\). The observed law then has the disintegration\[P(dx,\{s\},\{k\},dy)=P_X(dx)p_s(x)e_k(x,s)\kappa_{sk}(x,dy).\]

For \(m\in[a,b]\), the endpoint kernel satisfies\[\int y\,\nu_m(dy)=a\frac{b-m}{b-a}+b\frac{m-a}{b-a}=m.\]Moreover, \(m\mapsto\nu_m(B)\) is Borel measurable for every Borel \(B\subseteq[a,b]\), so it is a probability kernel. Define conditional laws of the potential-outcome pair by\[\begin{aligned}\Lambda_{1k}(x,dy_0,dy_1)&=\kappa_{10}(x,dy_0)\kappa_{11}(x,dy_1),\qquad k\in\{0,1\},\\\Lambda_{00}(x,dy_0,dy_1)&=\kappa_{00}(x,dy_0)\nu_{c_1^*(x)}(dy_1),\\\Lambda_{01}(x,dy_0,dy_1)&=\nu_{c_0^*(x)}(dy_0)\kappa_{01}(x,dy_1).\end{aligned}\]These are measurable probability kernels. Now define \(Q^*\) on \((X,S,A,Y(0),Y(1))\) by\[Q^*(dx,\{s\},\{k\},dy_0,dy_1)=P_X(dx)p_s(x)e_k(x,s)\Lambda_{sk}(x,dy_0,dy_1).\]

For every \(s,k\), the \(k\)-th marginal of \(\Lambda_{sk}\) is \(\kappa_{sk}\). Consequently, the law of \((X,S,A,Y(A))\) under \(Q^*\) is exactly \(P\), proving the pushforward restriction. In study one, \(\Lambda_{1k}=\kappa_{10}\otimes\kappa_{11}\) does not depend on \(k\); hence\[(Y(0),Y(1))\perp_{Q^*}A\mid(X,S=1).\]In study zero, treatment positivity and the mean identity for \(\nu_m\) give\[E_{Q^*}\{Y(t)\mid X,S=0,A=1-t\}=c_t^*(X).\]Thus relative NUC holds. A direct conditioning calculation gives\[\begin{aligned}E_{Q^*}\{Y(t)\mid X,S=0\}&=e_t(X,0)\mu_{0t}(X)+e_{1-t}(X,0)c_t^*(X)\\&=q_t^*(X),\end{aligned}\]and membership of \(q_t^*\) in the \(V_t\)-interval proves study exchangeability. Hence \(Q^*\in\mathcal M(P,\rho,\gamma)\), establishing sufficiency.

The two directions prove the equivalence. The displayed equality for \(\mathcal K(P)\) follows from its definition as the zero set of \(C_P\). At no step was a unique active face required, so endpoint ties, lower-maximum ties, upper-minimum ties, and outer-arm ties are all included."}
